summary: FASTA header format incompatible with search engine protein ID parser (critical). PSM-level FDR only — protein-level FDR missing. Percolator rescoring model trained on mismatched organism data.

findings.title: Only PSM-level FDR applied — protein-level FDR missing
findings.message: search_config.yaml configures FDR control at PSM level only (psm_fdr: 0.01). No peptide-level or protein-level FDR is applied. With a custom proteogenomics database of 112,400 entries (4.7x reference size), PSM-only FDR is insufficient: passing PSMs do not guarantee protein-level identification quality. Expected 5-10% false positive protein rate at PSM 1% threshold with this database size.
findings.impact: Protein list inflated by false positive identifications. Variant proteins especially affected: novel peptides have higher prior probability of being decoy matches in inflated databases. Differential expression analysis operates on a contaminated protein list.
findings.recommendation: Enable protein-level FDR at 1% in addition to PSM FDR. Use MaxQuant protein FDR or PeptideShaker protein scoring. For proteogenomics databases, implement class-specific FDR (reference vs variant class separately).
findings.code_snippet:
# search_config.yaml
fdr_control:
  psm_fdr: 0.01
  # MISSING: peptide_fdr and protein_fdr
  protein_fdr: null
references: Kall L et al. (2008) Posterior error probabilities and false discovery rates: two sides of the same coin. J Proteome Res 7(1):40-44

findings.title: FASTA header format incompatible with MaxQuant protein ID parser
findings.message: Custom proteogenomics FASTA uses header format '>VAR_{transcript_id}_{aa_change}' (e.g., '>VAR_ENST00000366667_p.Val600Glu'). MaxQuant expects UniProt-style headers: '>sp|P15056|BRAF_HUMAN' or '>tr|...' with a defined delimiter structure. The protein ID parser extracts the protein name as everything between the first '|' characters. For custom headers without '|' delimiters, MaxQuant falls back to using the entire header string as the protein ID, which breaks protein grouping: proteins with different header strings but identical sequences are not grouped, and proteins that should be grouped by gene name are not.
findings.impact: Protein grouping (protein families, leading proteins) is silently wrong. Quantification is aggregated at the wrong granularity. Protein-level FDR calibration is corrupted. Variant proteins cannot be disambiguated from reference proteins in MaxQuant output tables.
findings.recommendation: Reformat FASTA headers to UniProt-compatible format: '>VAR|{variant_id}|{gene_name}_HUMAN_{aa_change}' with pipe delimiters. Alternatively, use a custom FASTA header parsing rule in MaxQuant (protease.xml). Verify protein grouping output against expected variant/reference protein counts.
findings.code_snippet:
# Actual FASTA header (from proteogenomics pipeline)
>VAR_ENST00000366667_p.Val600Glu
# Expected by MaxQuant
>sp|P15056|BRAF_HUMAN BRAF serine/threonine-protein kinase OS=Homo sapiens

findings.title: Match-Between-Runs enabled without FDR assessment for transferred identifications
findings.message: MBR is enabled (match_between_runs: true, mbr_time_window: 0.7) for all samples. MBR transfers peptide identifications from runs where a peptide was directly sequenced to runs where only an apex is detected. No FDR framework exists for MBR transfers — all MBR identifications carry unknown false discovery probability.
findings.impact: MBR-only protein identifications (no MS/MS evidence in that sample) are included in downstream quantification and differential expression. For proteins identified exclusively via MBR in some samples, fold change estimates are unreliable. MBR inflation particularly affects low-abundance proteins.
findings.recommendation: Flag MBR-only identifications in MaxQuant output using 'Only identified by site' column. Exclude MBR-only proteins from differential expression analysis or apply more stringent thresholds. Consider disabling MBR for discovery proteogenomics where identification quality is critical.
findings.code_snippet:
mbr:
  enabled: true
  match_time_window: 0.7  # min
  match_ion_mobility_window: 0.05  # for TIMS data
  # No MBR-specific FDR or confidence threshold

findings.title: Quantile normalisation applied to TMT-labelled data
findings.message: quantification.R applies quantile normalisation to TMT reporter ion intensities. Quantile normalisation assumes that the overall distribution of protein abundance is identical across samples, which is appropriate for LFQ data but typically incorrect for TMT experiments — the mixing step for TMT already achieves sample-level normalisation. Applying quantile normalisation to TMT data may overcorrect, especially if one channel has genuine global abundance differences (e.g., different cell states or treatment conditions).
findings.impact: Systematic bias introduced in fold change estimates for proteins with genuine global abundance differences between conditions. Differential expression analysis will underestimate effect sizes for condition-wide expression changes.
findings.recommendation: For TMT data: use median centering or reference channel normalisation (normalize to internal standard or bridge channel if present). Reserve quantile normalisation for LFQ data. Verify normalisation choice in context of experimental design.

findings.title: Percolator rescoring model trained on yeast data applied to human peptides
findings.message: percolator_config.yaml specifies training_fasta: 'refs/yeast_training.fasta' for Percolator model training. The current dataset is human plasma proteomics on an Orbitrap Exploris 480. Percolator learns PSM quality features (charge state distribution, missed cleavages, mass accuracy) from training data. Yeast peptide length, charge state, and retention time distributions differ significantly from human plasma. The discriminant function will be miscalibrated for human peptides.
findings.impact: Percolator q-values and posterior error probabilities are incorrect. Some true human PSMs will be filtered out (miscalibrated threshold too strict); some false PSMs will pass (miscalibrated threshold too lenient). Net effect: both sensitivity and specificity of the final identification list are degraded.
findings.recommendation: Use a human training FASTA (e.g., a high-confidence human proteome identification from similar instrument and tissue type). If no suitable training set is available, run Percolator without a pre-trained model and let it learn from the data itself.
findings.code_snippet:
# percolator_config.yaml
training:
  fasta: 'refs/yeast_training.fasta'  # WRONG: human data
  instrument: 'orbitrap'
  fragmentation: 'HCD'

findings.title: PSM output table missing scan-level metadata for downstream validation
findings.message: PSM output table includes peptide sequence, protein, q-value, and intensity but omits: scan number, precursor m/z, charge state, and retention time. These fields are required for manual spectrum validation and for cross-referencing with raw data.
findings.impact: Manual validation of novel variant peptide identifications (required for proteogenomics publications) is impossible without scan number and precursor m/z. Raw data cross-reference is not possible.
findings.recommendation: Include scan_number, precursor_mz, charge, retention_time_min in PSM output. These fields are available from MaxQuant evidence.txt or PeptideShaker PSM output.
